lesson.title: MIG/MAG Welding (GMAW)
lesson.explanation: Gas Metal Arc Welding feeds continuous wire through a gun with shielding gas. MIG uses inert gas (argon) for aluminium/stainless. MAG uses active gas (CO2 or argon-CO2 mix) for mild steel. Higher deposition rates than SMAW — ideal for production. Wire sizes: 0.8mm (thin sheet), 1.0mm (general), 1.2mm (heavy). Settings: voltage controls arc length, wire speed controls heat input. Common in SA automotive, manufacturing, and fabrication. Produces cleaner welds than SMAW with less slag.

practice_problems.question: Set up a MIG welder for 3mm mild steel. Specify wire diameter, shielding gas, flow rate, wire feed speed, and voltage. Explain MIG vs MAG.
practice_problems.answer: Wire: 0.8mm. Gas: CO2 or 80%Ar/20%CO2. Flow: 12-15 L/min. Wire feed: 4-6 m/min. Voltage: 18-20V (short-circuit transfer). MIG = inert gas (pure Ar) for aluminium/stainless. MAG = active gas (CO2 or mix) for mild steel -- CO2 reacts with pool, improving penetration. Ar/CO2 mix gives less spatter but costs more. For SA mild steel, MAG with CO2 is most common.
hints: Correct MIG sounds like frying bacon. Crackling = voltage too low. Hissing = too high.
Wire feed and voltage must be balanced together.
practice_problems.explanation: Machine setup is the foundation of good MIG/MAG welding. Most production defects come from incorrect settings, not poor technique.
distractors: Use the thickest wire for maximum strength

practice_problems.question: After welding a long seam on a 2mm enclosure, you see 8mm of warping. Explain the cause and three prevention techniques.
practice_problems.answer: Cause: weld metal contracts as it cools, pulling plates inward (angular distortion). 2mm material has little stiffness to resist. Prevention: (1) Tack weld at 50-100mm intervals first to lock the joint. (2) Backstep welding: weld 100-150mm sections in reverse direction to distribute heat. (3) Pre-set: clamp panels 3-5° outward before welding so contraction pulls them straight. Additional: minimum heat input, alternate sides, weld centre-outward.
hints: Think of distortion as metal 'pulling itself together' as weld cools.
Thinner material distorts dramatically without countermeasures.
practice_problems.explanation: Distortion control is critical in production MIG welding. Understanding thermal expansion/contraction physics enables prediction and prevention.
distractors: Heat the other side to fix it

practice_problems.question: You are MIG welding 6mm 304 stainless steel. What specific setup and technique differences apply vs mild steel?
practice_problems.answer: Key differences: (1) Gas: 98%Ar/2%CO2 or tri-mix -- pure CO2 causes carbon pickup and sensitisation (destroys corrosion resistance). (2) Wire: ER308LSi. (3) Reduce amperage 10-15% -- stainless has lower thermal conductivity. (4) Inter-pass temp <150°C. (5) Use DEDICATED stainless tools (brushes, discs, clamps) -- carbon steel contamination ruins corrosion resistance. (6) Back-purge with argon for critical root welds.
hints: Stainless welding = preserving corrosion resistance. Wrong gas/tools defeats the purpose.
Cross-contamination from carbon steel is the most common failure cause.
practice_problems.explanation: Stainless MIG welding is specialist work. All differences relate to maintaining corrosion resistance.
distractors: Use same CO2 as mild steel
Same wire brush for both metals

practice_problems.question: Your MIG welder produces excessive spatter, harsh arc, and porosity. Diagnose systematically using the four main parameters.
practice_problems.answer: Four parameters: (1) Wire feed speed (WFS): too high = wire stubs into pool (harsh sound). Test: increase voltage -- if arc stabilises, WFS was too high. (2) Voltage: too low = stubbing and spatter. Adjust in 0.5V increments for smooth sizzle. (3) Gas flow: porosity = shielding failure. Check bottle, regulator (12-15 L/min), nozzle for spatter blockage, drafts. (4) Stick-out: 10-15mm correct; too long = unstable arc. Most likely for all three symptoms: gas flow problem (porosity) + voltage too low (spatter, harsh arc). Fix gas first, then balance WFS/voltage.
hints: Check gas FIRST -- most common porosity cause.
practice_problems.explanation: Systematic troubleshooting: address most likely cause first (gas for porosity), then fine-tune electrical parameters.
distractors: Porosity is always from dirty metal

practice_problems.question: A gate workshop uses CO2 at R800/cylinder. A supplier offers 80/20 Ar/CO2 at R1,400. Analyse the cost-benefit for decorative gate fabrication.
practice_problems.answer: 80/20 costs 75% more per cylinder but: (1) 50-70% less spatter = ~1 hour/day less grinding. At R200/hr labour, that's R200/day saved. (2) 10-15% faster welding, fewer rejects. (3) Smoother appearance = fewer cosmetic grinding passes (critical for visible gates). (4) Less fume. Break-even: extra cost ~R240/day (R600/cylinder / 2.5 days), saving ~R200/day in labour. Nearly break-even on cost alone, but quality improvement and health benefits tip in favour of 80/20 for decorative work. For heavy structural where appearance doesn't matter, CO2 remains more cost-effective.
hints: Cheapest gas is not always cheapest overall -- factor in labour and rework.
For decorative work, less grinding = lower total cost.
practice_problems.explanation: Gas selection is a business decision. Total welding cost = gas + wire + labour + rework.
distractors: Gas is the largest cost component

misconceptions.misconception: You don't need formal training for MIG/MAG Welding (GMAW)
misconceptions.correction: Proper training and certification are required for safety and legal compliance in SA.
misconceptions.why_students_think_this: People see informal work and assume no qualifications are needed.
